beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to build an MCP server, create an MCP, make an MCP integration, or wrap an API for Claude — the Build MCP Server skill fires. It is a discovery-and-routing skill: it asks four questions first, then hands off to a specialized skill like build-mcp-app or build-mcpb. Discovery-before-code is the whole model — the wrong deployment choice early means a painful rewrite. Let me walk you through the three deployment paths, the two tool-design patterns, and where this skill makes the decision easy and where the caveats hide.

props.command: Build an MCP server that wraps the GitHub API.
output: Discovery first: 4 questions before any scaffolding
Remote HTTP default — MCPB if local machine required — stdio prototype only
Pattern A: one-per-action · Pattern B: search+execute for large surfaces

beats.act: anatomy — three deployment models + decision matrix
beats.narration_text: There are three deployment paths, ranked by preference. Remote HTTP wins by default for anything wrapping a cloud API: zero install friction, one server serves all users, and OAuth flows work properly with redirect handling. Recommend this unless the server must touch the user's local machine. MCPB — Model Context Protocol Bundle — packages a server with its runtime for local distribution: the sanctioned way to ship when the server reads local files, drives a desktop app, or talks to localhost services. Local stdio via npx or uvx is fine for personal prototypes but painful to distribute — recommend it only as a stepping stone and flag the MCPB upgrade path. The decision matrix covers seven scenarios: small SaaS API goes remote, large SaaS goes remote with search-plus-execute, UI widgets go MCP app, local desktop goes MCPB.

props.sparkLine: Remote HTTP default. MCPB for local. stdio for prototypes only.

beats.narration_text: Tool design has two patterns. Pattern A: one tool per action. When the action space is under about fifteen operations, give each a dedicated tool with a tight description and schema. Claude reads the list once and knows exactly what's possible. Pattern B: search-plus-execute. When wrapping a large API — dozens to hundreds of endpoints — listing every operation floods the context window and degrades model performance. Expose two tools: search-actions to return matching operations for a natural-language intent, and execute-action to run an operation by ID. Context stays lean. The hybrid option promotes the three to five most-used actions to dedicated tools, keeping the long tail behind search and execute. Beyond tools, the other three primitives are resources — host-browsable docs and data — prompts — user-triggered slash commands — and elicitation — spec-native mid-tool user input without building UI. Most servers only need tools; knowing the others prevents reinventing wheels.

props.sparkLine: Under 15 tools: one per action. Large surface: search+execute. Most servers only need tools.

beats.act: teardown — what it gets right / where it bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. Build MCP Server gets five things right. The discovery-before-code structure — five questions first, no scaffolding until answered — stops the most common mistake of picking a deployment model before understanding the use case. The remote HTTP default is opinionated with a clear unless rule: unless the server must touch the local machine, remote wins. The search-plus-execute pattern for large API surfaces is documented with a concrete two-tool interface. Elicitation is correctly framed as spec-native, zero UI code, and the caveat about host support is stated. The seven-scenario decision matrix gives Claude a complete picture for any plausible combination. Where it bites. The elicitation capability check requirement is buried mid-paragraph: if you miss it and the client does not advertise the capability, the SDK throws with no clear error path. The FastMCP version disambiguation — jlowin's PyPI package versus the frozen 1.0 bundled in the official mcp SDK — is a single sentence and easy to skip, but using the wrong package breaks decorator-based routing silently. Tool description quality is referenced out to references-slash-tool-design-dot-md with bare inline guidance, not the signal-to-schema advice that matters. The CIMD versus DCR distinction for OAuth is mentioned but fully deferred to references-slash-auth-dot-md. And the load Claude docs first invariant at the top has no enforcement — the Phase 1 questions follow immediately, so a reader can skip the fetch and proceed on stale assumptions.

props.sparkLine: Discovery-before-code and remote-HTTP default solid. Elicitation check and FastMCP split: surface them.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. Build MCP Server: discovery-before-code, four questions, then hand off to build-mcp-app or build-mcpb. Remote HTTP default — unless server must touch local machine. MCPB for local: filesystem, desktop apps, localhost. Local stdio: prototype only. Pattern A: one per action under fifteen tools. Pattern B: search-plus-execute for large surfaces. Elicitation: spec-native mid-tool input — capability check required or SDK throws. Gaps: elicitation capability check buried, FastMCP version split, tool descriptions deferred, OAuth details in references only.

artifactLines: Discovery: 4 questions before scaffolding — deployment, users, action count, auth
Remote HTTP default · MCPB for local machine · stdio prototype only
Pattern A: one per action <15 · Pattern B: search+execute for large surfaces
Elicitation: spec-native, zero UI — capability check required or SDK throws
Frameworks: TypeScript SDK (default) · FastMCP 3.x (jlowin PyPI, not frozen 1.0)
Gaps: elicitation check buried · FastMCP version split · tool desc deferred · OAuth refs-only

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude Code session. Paste this: Build an MCP server that wraps the GitHub API — tools for creating issues, searching repositories, and getting pull request details. Watch four things. First: does it ask deployment questions before scaffolding, or does it start generating code immediately? Second: does it recommend remote HTTP as the default and give a reason? Third: does it use one tool per action since the surface is small — or does it mistakenly apply search-plus-execute to three tools? Fourth: are the tool descriptions specific enough that Claude could choose between create-issue and update-issue without ambiguity? A description that just restates the tool name is a signal it did not apply the tool-design guidance.

props.command: Build an MCP server that wraps the GitHub API — create issues, search repos, get PRs.
props.runningText: Watch: discovery first? remote HTTP? one-per-action? specific descriptions?
output: Expected: deployment questions before any code · remote HTTP recommended
Expected: one tool per action (3 tools, not search+execute) · descriptions specific
Red flag: immediate scaffolding · search+execute for 3 tools · descriptions restate name

beats.narration_text: Build MCP Server. Liam, in for Bear.
